title: Light a Candle for Them
subtitle: A ten-minute ritual for a quiet act of remembrance
excerpt: A ten-minute ritual for grief. One candle, a chair, and the choice to sit with the person you are missing for a small marked-out window. Used on days when the missing is loud and there is nowhere else to put it.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Lighting a candle for a person who has died is a custom in many faiths and in many cultures.

practice.whenToUse: Use on days when the missing is loud and there is nowhere else to put it. Also workable as a regular weekly act of remembrance.

content.text: A ten-minute ritual for grief. The ritual is small: one candle, one chair, ten minutes. The candle gives the missing somewhere to go for a short marked-out window.
New to ritual as a practice? Read
how rituals work
first.
What you will need
A candle. A safe surface. A chair you can sit in for ten minutes. A picture of them if you have one; not needed if you do not.
The practice
Prepare
Sit somewhere quiet. Put the candle on the surface in front of you. If you have a picture of them, set it next to the candle. Take one slow breath.
Release (repeat x3)
I am ready to release the wall I have been holding up to get through the day. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
Allow (repeat x3)
I am ready to allow these ten minutes for the missing. I allow them now. I allow them now. I allow them now.
Integrate
Light the candle. Sit with it for the rest of the ten minutes. You can talk to them, in your head or out loud. You can cry. You can sit quietly. You can say nothing.
There is no right thing to feel in the ten minutes. The candle is the witness. You are not on your own with the missing.
Ground it
I miss them. They are still part of me.
Say it once before you blow the candle out. Then stand up and carry on with the day.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. Lighting a candle for a person who has died is a custom in many faiths and in many cultures.
